all.title: Formal-system-adaptive conjecture verification for multi-agent discovery systems
all.rationale: All branches (A–H) inherit the assumption of a fixed formal environment (Lean, linear algebra, group theory) for verification, blocking the ability to adapt to new mathematical domains. This shared residual gap is the most pressing bottleneck—the field has decentralized conjecture generation and proof attempts but remains tethered to a single formal system. Solving it would enable multi-agent systems to operate across arbitrary formalisms without manual reconfiguration.
all.meta_gap: The multi-agent system does not establish a formal-system-adaptive conjecture verification property; it inherits from all ancestors the assumption that a fixed formal environment (Lean, linear algebra, group theory) is available for verification or evaluation.
all.shared_mechanism: All branches assume a fixed formal system (e.g., Lean, linear algebra axioms) for proof verification, preventing the system from generalizing to domains where no such pre-built environment exists.
all.non_obvious_property: Property: Formal-system-agnostic conjecture verification that does not require a pre-defined proof assistant. Obvious composition that fails: Multi-agent RL + a formal proof assistant (e.g., Lean Copilot + Aristotle). This fails because the formal assistant is hard-coded; the system cannot switch to a different logic or axiom set without retraining. Why prior work has not formalized this property: Prior work treats formal verification as a fixed infrastructure (e.g., Lean, group theory axioms) rather than a learnable component of the discovery loop.

all.title: Decentralized feedback-driven discovery with adaptive formal verification
all.rationale: Multiple branches (A–F) have recently taken actions to decentralize conjecture generation and introduce feedback loops (structural changes from single-agent to multi-agent, reasoning_pattern from static to feedback-driven). The collective trajectory is toward fully autonomous, multi-agent discovery. However, the residual gap of fixed formal verification remains. The implied next problem after this trajectory completes is how to make the verification mechanism itself adaptive to new formal systems—so that the decentralized feedback loop can operate across diverse mathematical domains.
all.trend_endpoint: A fully autonomous multi-agent system that generates and verifies conjectures via collective learning
all.implied_next_problem: Enabling the verification component to adapt to arbitrary formal systems without human intervention, overcoming the fixed-environment assumption inherited from all ancestors.
all.non_obvious_property: Property: Co-adaptation of conjecture generation and verification mechanisms across formal systems. Obvious composition that fails: Multi-agent RL + automated theorem prover (e.g., E prover, Vampire). This fails because the prover is still domain-specific (e.g., first-order logic) and does not learn to adapt its own inference rules. Why prior work has not formalized this property: Prior work externalizes verification to fixed tools, treating them as non-learnable oracles.
